attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Generated from research/people dossiers; for maintainer + Tarot History Forum review.

name: People & Institutions of Tarot — The Hands Behind the Cards
description: The makers, patrons, occultists, scholars, and institutions behind the historical tarot decks in this collection. Each node links to the deck(s) that person or body made, reframed, printed, or holds — and, where specific, to the cards that feature them (e.g. Pamela Colman Smith's scenes in the RWS deck). Generated from the research/people dossiers, which carry the sourced evidence.

sections.Who:
A cataloguer at the New York Public Library for some four decades, Gertrude Moakley produced
the decisive iconographic insight of modern tarot history: the 22 trumps are a **procession
of triumphs** in the manner of **Petrarch's _I Trionfi_** and the carnival *trionfo* — Love,
Chastity, Death, Fame, Time, Eternity — not a coded Egyptian or kabbalistic book. Her reading
grounded the trumps in the actual visual culture of 15th-century Italy.
sections.Claim vs the record:
Moakley **dissolved** the occult origin-myth on iconographic grounds before Dummett
systematised the documentary case: the trumps are intelligible as Renaissance triumph-imagery,
so no Egyptian or Hebrew key is required to explain them. Her attribution to **Bonifacio
Bembo** is itself now hedged (the Zavattari workshop is a rival candidate) — preserve the
hedge.
sections.In this collection:
Not a maker — the **iconographer**. Cited in the Visconti-Sforza and Cary-Yale dossiers and
in the trump-meaning notes wherever the "triumph procession" reading is invoked.

sections.Who:
Leo Aryeh Mayer was an Israeli scholar of Islamic art who, in the 1930s, recognised a set
of richly decorated cards in the Topkapı Sarayı, Istanbul, as a **Mamluk-era playing pack** —
the *Mulūk wa-nuwwāb* ("Kings and Deputies"). His identification supplied the missing link
between Chinese money-suited cards and the European four-suit pack that became tarot.
sections.Claim vs the record:
Mayer's careful description corrected loose talk of "Saracen" or "gypsy" card origins with a
dated, physical object. Note the survivor-count nuance his work and its readers established:
the Topkapı set is **almost complete (~48 cards)**, of which **~43 belong to the original
pack** and a handful are cruder later replacements; the complete pack held 52. (confidence:
high on the structure; the exact survivor count is reported variously as 43/47/48 across
sources — the dossier states the reconciled figure.)
sections.In this collection:
Not a maker — the **identifier** of the `mamluk-deck` ancestor. He is the principal citation
in the Mamluk deck dossier and grammar.

sections.Who:
One of the major analytic philosophers of the 20th century (Wykeham Professor of Logic at
Oxford), Michael Dummett was also the most important modern historian of tarot — not as a
divinatory system but as a **trick-taking card game**. His trilogy is the documentary
baseline this collection defaults to: tarot is a 15th-century Italian game; the
occult/Egyptian/divinatory readings are a learned **overlay imposed after c. 1781**.
sections.Claim vs the record:
Dummett **is** the record here. Where a deck's own tradition claims ancient Egyptian,
Hebrew-kabbalistic, or "suppressed wisdom" origins, this collection states the claim fairly
and then weighs it against Dummett's documentary findings. The collection does **not** treat
him as infallible — later scholars (Depaulis, Pratesi, Vitali) refine dates and the A/B/C
picture — but it treats the burden of proof as his framework sets it.
sections.In this collection:
Not a maker — a **lens**. He is cited across nearly every deck dossier as the document-first
authority (`[@dummett1980]`, `[@decker1996]`, `[@dummett2004]`). He does not "feature" in
card art; he features in the collection's *method*.

sections.Who:
Stuart Kaplan founded **U.S. Games Systems** (1968) and, more than anyone, drove tarot's
late-20th-century popular revival in the United States. As a collector-cataloguer he compiled
the four-volume **_Encyclopedia of Tarot_**, the most comprehensive descriptive catalogue of
historical decks; as a publisher he reissued the **Rider-Waite-Smith** deck and many others.
sections.Claim vs the record:
Kaplan is a **catalogue** authority more than an origins theorist; his Encyclopedia is
descriptive and is used here for *what a deck is and where copies are*, not for contested
origin claims (for those, defer to the Dummett spine). His commercial framing sometimes
carries the occult tradition's self-description — read those passages as tradition, not as
documented history.
sections.In this collection:
Not a historical maker — a **cataloguer/publisher**. Cited where a dossier needs deck
identification, provenance of surviving copies, or the modern publication history of the RWS
line.

sections.What this groups: 4 scholars & cataloguers catalogued in this collection.

sections.What it is: Every person and institution that made, paid for, reframed, printed, or catalogued the decks in this collection — generated from the research/people dossiers.
